en:
On analytical investigation it becomes plain that the dream, as we remember it, is only a façade which conceals the contents within the house. But if, observing certain technical rules, we get the dreamer to talk about the details of his dream, it soon appears that his free associations group themselves in certain directions and round certain topics. These appear to be of personal significance, and have a meaning which at first sight would not be suspected. Careful comparison shows that they are in close and subtle symbolic connection with the dream-façade. [230] This particular complex of ideas in which all the threads of the dream unite, is the conflict for which we are seeking; is its particular form at the moment, conditioned by the immediate circumstances. What is painful and incompatible is in this way so covered up or split that we can call it a wish-fulfilment; but we must immediately add that the wishes fulfilled in the dream do not seem at first sight to be our wishes, but rather the very opposite. For instance, a daughter loves her mother tenderly, but she dreams that her mother is dead; this causes her great grief. Such dreams, where apparently there is no trace of any wish-fulfilment are innumerable, and are a constant stumbling-block to our learned critics, for— incredible dictu —they still cannot grasp the simple distinction between the manifest and the latent content of the dream. We must guard against such an error; the conflict dealt with in the dream is an unconscious one, and equally so also is the manner its solution. Our dreamer has, as a matter of fact, the wish to get away from her mother—expressed in the language of the unconscious, she wants her mother to die. Now we know that a certain section of the unconscious contains all our lost memories, and also all those infantile impulses that cannot find any application in adult life—a series, that is, of ruthless childish desires. We may say that for the most part the unconscious bears an infantile stamp; like the child's simple wish: "Daddy, when Mummie is dead, will you marry me?" In a dream that infantile expression of a wish is the substitute for a recent wish to marry, which is painful to the dreamer for reasons still undiscovered. This thought, or rather the seriousness of its corresponding intention, is said to be "repressed into the unconscious" and must there necessarily express itself in an infantile way, for the material which is at the disposal of the unconscious consists chiefly of infantile memories. As the latest researches of the Zürich school have shown, [231] these are not only infantile memories but also "racial" memories, extending far beyond the limits of individual existence.

Important desires which have not been sufficiently gratified, or have been "repressed," during the day find their symbolic substitution in dreams. Because moral tendencies usually predominate in waking hours, these ungratified desires which strive to realise themselves symbolically in the dream are, as rule, erotic ones. It is, therefore, somewhat rash to tell dreams before one who understands, for the symbolism is often extremely transparent to him who knows the rules! The clearest in this respect are "anxiety-dreams" which are so common, and which invariably symbolise a strong erotic desire.

Often the dream apparently deals with quite irrelevant details, thereby making a ridiculous impression; or else it is so unintelligible that we are simply amazed at it, and accordingly have to overcome considerable resistance in ourselves before we can set to work seriously to unravel its symbolic weaving by patient work. But when at last we penetrate into its real meaning we find ourselves at a bound in the very heart of the dreamer's secrets, and find to our astonishment that an apparently senseless dream is quite full of sense, and deals with extraordinarily important and serious problems of the soul. Having acquired this knowledge we cannot refrain from giving rather more credit to the old superstitions concerning the meaning of dreams for which our rationalising tendencies, until lately, had no use.
zh:
在分析性的探究中，我們會清楚發現，夢——如同我們所記得的——僅是一層外觀，遮蔽了屋內所含的內容。然而，若我們遵循某些技術規則，讓做夢者談論其夢境的細節，很快就會發現，他的自由聯想會朝特定方向、圍繞特定主題聚集。這些主題似乎具有個人意義，且含有一種乍看之下不會被懷疑的意涵。仔細比較後顯示，它們與夢的外觀之間存在著緊密而微妙的象徵性連結。[230] 這個特定的觀念叢結——夢的所有線索在此匯聚——正是我們所尋找的衝突；它是當下因直接環境所制約的特定形式。痛苦與不相容的事物便以這種方式被掩蓋或分裂，以致我們可以稱之為願望滿足；但我們必須立即補充：在夢中實現的願望，乍看之下似乎並非我們的願望，反而恰恰相反。例如，一位女兒深愛她的母親，卻夢見母親死了；這使她極度悲傷。這類夢——顯然毫無願望滿足的痕跡——不計其數，並且一直是我們那些學究式批評者的絆腳石，因為——說來難以置信——他們仍然無法掌握夢的顯意與隱意之間的簡單區別。我們必須防範此類錯誤；夢中所處理的衝突是無意識的，其解決方式也同樣是無意識的。事實上，我們這位做夢者懷有脫離母親的願望——以無意識的語言來說，她希望母親死去。現在我們知道，無意識的某個部分包含了我們所有遺失的記憶，以及所有那些在成人生活中無法找到應用的嬰兒衝動——也就是一系列無情的孩童慾望。我們可以說，無意識大部分帶有嬰兒的印記；就像孩子單純的願望：「爸爸，等媽媽死了，你會娶我嗎？」在夢中，這種願望的嬰兒式表達，取代了一個近期的結婚願望——這個願望因尚未發現的原因而令做夢者感到痛苦。這個念頭，或者說其相應意圖的嚴肅性，據說被「壓抑到無意識中」，並且必須在那裡以嬰兒方式表達出來，因為無意識所能運用的材料主要由嬰兒記憶構成。正如蘇黎世學派的最新研究所顯示的，[231] 這些不僅是嬰兒記憶，還包括「種族」記憶，遠遠超出個體存在的界限。

白天未能充分滿足或被「壓抑」的重要慾望，在夢中找到了它們的象徵性替代。由於道德傾向通常在清醒時佔主導地位，這些未獲滿足、試圖在夢中象徵性地實現自身的慾望，通常都是情慾的。因此，在懂得解夢的人面前述說夢境是有點魯莽的，因為對懂得規則的人來說，象徵往往極其透明！在這方面最清楚的是「焦慮之夢」——它們非常普遍，並且總是象徵著強烈的情慾慾望。

夢常常看似處理完全無關緊要的細節，從而給人荒謬的印象；或者它如此難以理解，以至於我們對它感到驚奇，因此必須克服自身相當大的抗拒，才能開始認真地透過耐心工作來解開其象徵編織。但當我們最終深入其真實意義時，我們會發現自己一躍進入了做夢者秘密的核心，並驚訝地發現，一個看似無意義的夢其實充滿了意義，並且處理著靈魂中極其重要且嚴肅的問題。獲得了這項知識後，我們不禁要對那些關於夢之意義的古老迷信給予更多信任——而我們理性化的傾向直到最近都還認為這些迷信毫無用處。
